natural_occurrence.description: A water-soluble dietary fiber extracted from the corm (bulb) of the konjac plant, native to Southeast Asia. Traditional Japanese food for over 1,000 years, known as konnyaku.

manufacturing.description: Konjac corms are harvested, washed, dried, and ground into flour. Glucomannan is extracted and purified from the flour. The gel form requires treatment with alkali (calcium hydroxide) to form a stable gel structure.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used in facial cleansing sponges (konjac sponges) and as a thickener in skincare products.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Sold as a dietary fiber supplement for weight loss, cholesterol reduction, and blood sugar management.
other_uses.household: Traditional Japanese New Year food (osechi ryori)

eu.conditions: Approved with restrictions. Mini-cup jelly containing konjac is banned due to choking hazard.
eu.legal_reference: Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008, Annex II; Commission Decision 2004/374/EC (konjac ban in mini-cups)

usa.notes: FDA issued warnings about konjac candy/gel confections due to choking hazard. Import alerts in place for mini-cup jelly products.

japan.notes: Traditional food with centuries of use. Warnings issued about choking hazards, especially for children and elderly.

australia_nz.notes: Konjac mini-cup jellies banned due to choking risk

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Emulsifying, Gelling, Stabilizing or Thickening Agents
canada.notes: Health Canada has issued warnings about konjac candy choking hazards

regulatory_history.event: FDA issues import alert and consumer warning about konjac candy choking hazards after multiple deaths
EU bans konjac in mini-cup jelly confections (Commission Decision 2004/374/EC)
